questions.question: Why is standard DNS a privacy problem?
options: DNS responses contain your IP address in the payload
DNS queries are sent in plaintext, so anyone on the network path can read what domains you visit
DNS uses TCP, which is easier to intercept than UDP
DNS servers log all queries by law in most countries
questions.explanation: Standard DNS on port 53 is unencrypted. Your ISP, network admin, and anyone who can observe your traffic can read every domain you resolve. DNS-over-HTTPS wraps queries in TLS so the query is encrypted and looks like normal HTTPS traffic.

questions.question: Why can't apps on your computer directly use a DoH server without a local proxy?
options: DoH requires a special OS driver to function
Most apps and the OS DNS resolver use UDP port 53, not HTTPS — they don't speak DoH natively
DoH is only available on Linux and macOS
DoH responses are incompatible with the DNS record format apps expect
questions.explanation: The OS resolver and most apps are hardcoded to send UDP queries to port 53. They have no knowledge of HTTPS. A local DoH proxy bridges this gap: it listens on UDP port 53 (or 5353 for testing), speaks plain DNS to local apps, and translates queries to HTTPS for the upstream resolver.

questions.question: What is base64url encoding and why does DoH use it instead of standard base64?
options: base64url is faster to encode/decode than standard base64
base64url replaces + and / with - and _ so the encoded string is safe in a URL query parameter
base64url compresses the DNS query to reduce request size
base64url is required by TLS for binary payloads
questions.explanation: Standard base64 uses + and / which are special characters in URLs (+ means space, / is a path separator). base64url substitutes these with - and _, and drops the = padding, so the encoded DNS query can appear in a ?dns= query parameter without percent-encoding corruption.

questions.question: In the DoH GET request, what does `Accept: application/dns-message` tell the server?
options: The client can accept compressed DNS responses
The client wants the response in raw binary DNS wire format, not JSON
The client is a DNS resolver, not a browser
The client accepts any content type the server prefers
questions.explanation: DoH supports both raw binary (application/dns-message) and JSON (application/dns-json) response formats. Setting Accept: application/dns-message tells the DoH server to return the response in standard DNS wire format — the same binary format your stub resolver already parses from Phase 4.

questions.question: When the proxy cannot reach the DoH server, what DNS response code should it return to the client?
options: NXDOMAIN (3) — domain not found
FORMERR (1) — malformed query
SERVFAIL (2) — server failure
REFUSED (5) — policy rejection
questions.explanation: SERVFAIL (RCODE=2) means the server encountered a problem it couldn't resolve — which accurately describes 'I can't reach the upstream DoH server'. NXDOMAIN would falsely claim the domain doesn't exist. REFUSED means a policy decision, not a connectivity failure.
